title: SEGMENT 13 — T2T-CHM13 in 2022: the truly complete genome

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — T2T-CHM13 in 2022: the truly complete genome
I told you in Segment 9 that the 2003 "finished" genome was not actually finished.

About eight percent of it — roughly two hundred million bases — was left unassembled, in the centromeres, the telomeres, the long arms of the acrocentric chromosomes, and various other heterochromatic regions.
Those gaps stayed gaps for nearly twenty years, because the short-read sequencing technologies of the 2000s and 2010s could not span the long, near-identical repeat arrays that fill those regions.
The technology that finally cracked the problem is long-read sequencing.

Two platforms in particular: PacBio HiFi, which uses single-molecule real-time circular consensus sequencing to produce reads of fifteen to twenty kilobases at greater than 99.9 percent accuracy, and Oxford Nanopore, which threads single DNA molecules through a protein pore and measures the electrical current changes as each base passes through, producing reads of one hundred kilobases to over a megabase, with accuracy improving steadily into the high-99-percent range.
Combined, these technologies can read across centromeric satellite arrays that are hundreds of kilobases long, made up of a single repeat unit copied tens of thousands of times.
The 2003 short-read approach simply could not do this — the reads were too short to span the repeats, and the assembler could not figure out how many copies were present or in what order.
The Telomere-to-Telomere consortium, T2T, was formed around 2019 by an informal group of long-read sequencing experts, led by Adam Phillippy at NIH and Karen Miga at UC Santa Cruz.
They chose to focus on a single sample, called CHM13, which is a hydatidiform mole cell line.

A hydatidiform mole is an abnormal pregnancy in which a single sperm fertilizes an egg that has lost its own nucleus; the resulting cells are essentially haploid, with two identical copies of every chromosome.
Sequencing a haploid sample dramatically simplifies the assembly problem because you do not have to deal with two different parental haplotypes.

In April 2022, the T2T consortium published the first truly complete sequence of a human genome — every chromosome, end to end, including all centromeres, all telomeres, all acrocentric short arms, all of it.

T2T-CHM13 added approximately two hundred million new bases compared to the previous reference, corrected thousands of errors in the older assembly, and uncovered roughly two thousand new protein-coding gene candidates, mostly in regions that had been black boxes.
The analogy I want you to hold for T2T is this.

The 2003 reference was like a map of the United States that had every city, every highway, and every river — but with about eight percent of the map covered by clouds, because the satellite imagery could not see through them.
You knew where the clouds were, you knew roughly how big the regions were, but the actual terrain was invisible.
T2T finally lifted the clouds.
We can now see the centromeres — the structural anchors that hold chromosomes together during cell division.
We can see the telomere ends.
We can see the ribosomal DNA arrays.
None of these regions were terra incognita in the literal sense, but they had been sequence incognita for the entire history of human genomics.
The discoveries from T2T are still being worked out.
The centromeric satellite arrays turn out to have far more structural variation between individuals than anyone expected.
The acrocentric short arms contain unexpected gene families.
The regions that had been assumed to be inert repeat junk turn out to harbor active transcription and to vary between people in ways that may have functional consequences.
T2T-CHM13 is now widely used as the reference of choice for new analyses.
The old GRCh38 reference is still in heavy use because of inertia and tool compatibility, but the field is migrating.
By the end of the 2020s, T2T-CHM13 will likely be the standard.
